title: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
objectives: Recognize the classic presentation of SAH
Understand the diagnostic approach including LP

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Classic Presentation:**
• "Thunderclap headache" - worst headache of life
• Peak intensity within seconds to minutes
• Often associated with exertion, Valsalva
• Neck stiffness (may take hours to develop)
• Nausea, vomiting
• Photophobia
• Altered consciousness (25%)
• Focal neurological deficits

**Sentinel Headache:**
• Minor leak preceding major rupture
• Occurs in 20-50% of cases
• Often missed on initial presentation
• Average 2 weeks before major SAH

**Warning Signs:**
• Sudden onset is the key feature
• "First or worst" headache
• Different from usual headaches
• Syncope at onset (concerning for severe SAH)

sections.title: Etiology
sections.content:
**Aneurysmal SAH (85%):**
• Berry/saccular aneurysms most common
• Common locations:
  - Anterior communicating artery (30%)
  - Posterior communicating artery (25%)
  - Middle cerebral artery (20%)
• Risk factors: HTN, smoking, family history, polycystic kidney disease, connective tissue disorders

**Non-Aneurysmal (15%):**
• Perimesencephalic hemorrhage (benign, good prognosis)
• AVM rupture
• Trauma
• Dural AV fistula
• Vasculitis
• Cocaine use
• Coagulopathy

sections.title: Diagnostic Approach
sections.content:
**Step 1: Non-contrast CT Head**
• Sensitivity: 98% within 6 hours, 93% at 24 hours
• Decreases over time (50% at 1 week)
• Look for: Hyperdensity in basal cisterns, sylvian fissures, interhemispheric fissure

**Step 2: Lumbar Puncture (if CT negative)**
• Must wait 6-12 hours after symptom onset
• Allows RBC lysis and xanthochromia development
• Findings:
  - Elevated RBC count (not decreasing across tubes = true SAH)
  - Xanthochromia (yellow supernatant) - most specific
  - Elevated opening pressure

**Step 3: Vascular Imaging**
• CTA: First-line to identify aneurysm
• Sensitivity >95% for aneurysms >3mm
• Digital subtraction angiography (DSA): Gold standard
• MRA: Alternative if CTA contraindicated

**Clinical Pearl:**
Negative CT + negative LP effectively rules out SAH. Xanthochromia is KEY.

sections.title: Grading Scales
sections.content:
**Hunt-Hess Grade (Clinical):**
• Grade 1: Asymptomatic or mild headache
• Grade 2: Moderate headache, nuchal rigidity, CN palsy
• Grade 3: Drowsy, confused, mild focal deficit
• Grade 4: Stupor, moderate hemiparesis
• Grade 5: Coma, decerebrate posturing

**Fisher Grade (CT findings):**
• Grade 1: No blood detected
• Grade 2: Diffuse thin SAH (<1mm)
• Grade 3: Localized clot or thick SAH (>1mm)
• Grade 4: Intracerebral or intraventricular blood

**Prognostic Significance:**
• Higher Hunt-Hess = worse neurological outcome
• Fisher 3 = highest risk of vasospasm

sections.title: Complications
sections.content:
**Rebleeding:**
• Highest risk in first 24 hours
• 4% risk in first day, then 1-2% per day
• Mortality 70% with rebleeding
• Prevention: Early aneurysm securing, BP control

**Vasospasm:**
• Onset: Days 3-14 (peak day 7-10)
• Clinical: Delayed neurological deterioration
• Diagnosis: TCD, CTA
• Prevention: Nimodipine 60mg q4h x 21 days
• Treatment: Triple-H therapy (hypertension, hypervolemia, hemodilution) or endovascular intervention

**Hydrocephalus:**
• Acute: Blood blocks CSF drainage
• Chronic: Communicating hydrocephalus
• Treatment: EVD acutely, VP shunt if persistent

**Seizures:**
• 6-18% of patients
• Prophylaxis controversial (typically brief course)

**Hyponatremia:**
• SIADH or cerebral salt wasting
• CSW: Volume depleted (treat with fluids)
• SIADH: Euvolemic (fluid restrict carefully)

sections.title: Management
sections.content:
**Initial Stabilization:**
• ABCs, GCS monitoring
• BP control: Target SBP <160 until aneurysm secured
• Avoid hypotension (maintain CPP)
• Pain control (avoid aspirin/NSAIDs)
• Seizure precautions

**Aneurysm Treatment:**
• Goal: Secure within 24-72 hours
• Options:
  - Endovascular coiling (preferred for most)
  - Surgical clipping
• Decision based on aneurysm characteristics, patient factors

**Vasospasm Prevention/Treatment:**
• Nimodipine 60mg PO/NG q4h x 21 days
• Maintain euvolemia (avoid hypovolemia)
• If symptomatic vasospasm: Induced hypertension
• Endovascular options: Intra-arterial verapamil, angioplasty

**Supportive Care:**
• ICU monitoring
• DVT prophylaxis (SCDs initially, then heparin after securing)
• Glucose control
• Fever prevention/treatment
• Stool softeners (avoid Valsalva)

keyTakeaways: Thunderclap headache = SAH until proven otherwise
CT sensitivity decreases over time; LP needed if CT negative
Xanthochromia on LP is the key finding (requires 6-12h to develop)
Vasospasm peaks days 7-10; nimodipine is standard prevention
Rebleeding risk highest in first 24 hours—secure aneurysm early
Hunt-Hess grade correlates with outcome; Fisher grade predicts vasospasm

references.citation: Connolly ES, Rabinstein AA, Carhuapoma JR, et al. Guidelines for the Management of Aneurysmal Subarachnoid Hemorrhage. Stroke. 2012;43:1711-1737
Lawton MT, Vates GE. Subarachnoid Hemorrhage. N Engl J Med. 2017;377:257-266
references.keyContent: NEJM clinical review of presentation, diagnosis, management

references.citation: Perry JJ, Stiell IG, Sivilotti MLA, et al. Clinical Decision Rules to Rule Out SAH for Acute Headache. JAMA. 2013;310(12):1248-1255

references.keyContent: Hunt-Hess and Fisher scales, vasospasm management

reviewQuestions.question: A patient presents with sudden severe headache 3 hours ago. CT head is negative. What is the next step?
reviewQuestions.explanation: CT sensitivity for SAH decreases over time. A negative CT within 6 hours has ~98% sensitivity. LP should be performed but wait 6-12 hours from symptom onset for xanthochromia to develop.

reviewQuestions.question: A patient with confirmed SAH is on day 8 and develops new confusion and left arm weakness. What is the most likely complication?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Day 8 is peak time for vasospasm (days 3-14). New focal deficits with altered mental status during this window should prompt immediate evaluation with TCD or CTA and aggressive treatment.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected subarachnoid hemorrhage?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in subarachnoid hemorrhage. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of subarachnoid hemorrhage. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing subarachnoid hemorrhage, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
